did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses attention-based convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures (specifically based on the EfficientNet-B3 backbone) for the automated diagnosis and multi-class classification of melanoma and other skin lesions. The study proposes four novel lightweight deep learning models and evaluates their performance on dermatological image datasets (ISIC 2020, PROVe-AI, and HIBA) to detect melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the proposal of four novel lightweight attention-based CNN architectures for automated melanoma diagnosis. It details specific methodology (pre-processing, loss functions, model architectures), describes experiments conducted on independent test sets (PROVe-AI, HIBA), and reports new quantitative results (ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, sensitivity, specificity) comparing these new models against ISIC 2020 challenge winners. While it includes a related work section, its primary purpose is reporting new technical contributions and validation, not summarizing prior literature.

title: Going Smaller: Attention-based models for automated melanoma diagnosis
brief_summary: This study addresses the computational challenges of large AI models in automated melanoma diagnosis by proposing four lightweight, attention-based convolutional neural network architectures based on the EfficientNet-B3 backbone. The models were trained on a combined dataset of 58,457 images from the ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 challenges to perform multi-class classification of skin lesions. The authors evaluated their proposed models against the top three winners of the ISIC 2020 challenge using two independent test sets: PROVe-AI and HIBA. The results demonstrated that the novel models achieved performance comparable to the first-placed ISIC winner while using up to 98% fewer parameters. Specifically, the best-performing model on the PROVe-AI set achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.83, and on the HIBA set, it achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.91. These findings highlight the feasibility of deploying compact, efficient diagnostic models in resource-limited or point-of-care settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for automated diagnosis; included in training (5,106 samples) and test sets (PROVe-AI: 95, HIBA: 253).
Benign class; included in training (18,069 samples) and test sets (PROVe-AI: 340, HIBA: 602).
Skin cancer class; included in training (3,323 samples) and test sets (PROVe-AI: 9, HIBA: 340).
Benign class; included in training (2,847 samples) and test sets (PROVe-AI: 110, HIBA: 88).
Pre-cancerous lesion; included in training (867 samples) and test sets (PROVe-AI: 19, HIBA: 63).
Skin cancer class; included in training (628 samples) and test sets (PROVe-AI: 13, HIBA: 158).
Benign class; included in training (239 samples) and test sets (PROVe-AI: 11, HIBA: 61).
Benign class; included in training (253 samples) and test sets (PROVe-AI: 1, HIBA: 51).
Class from ISIC 2020 dataset; included in training (27,125 samples). Mapped to 'Other' in PROVe-AI test set.
Category used in PROVe-AI test set for images with labels not matching the predefined classes (5 samples).

all_task_types.target: Multi-class classification of skin lesions (Melanoma, Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Actinic Keratosis, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Unknown/Other)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the models perform 'multi-class classification of skin lesions' and 'nuanced classification of melanoma'. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) using attention-based CNN architectures. Performance is evaluated using metrics typical for classification such as ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 Combined Dataset
datasets_used.description: A combined training and validation dataset constructed from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2019 and ISIC 2020 challenges. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions with metadata. The study curated this dataset to address class imbalance and used it for model development.

use.training: Used for training the proposed attention-based CNN models. The dataset was split into five folds using k-fold cross-validation, with one fold used for validation and the remaining four for training in each iteration.
use.validation: Used for validation via k-fold cross-validation (one fold per iteration) to tune hyperparameters and select loss functions.
use.other: Used to construct the training and validation sets; not used for final independent testing.

datasets_used.name: PROVe-AI
datasets_used.description: An independent test set sourced from the ISIC archive, used to assess the real-world performance of the models. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.testing: Used as the first independent test set to evaluate the performance of the proposed models against the top three ISIC 2020 challenge winners.

datasets_used.name: HIBA (Hospital Italiano de Buenos Aires)
datasets_used.description: A second independent test set collected from Hospital Italiano de Buenos Aires. It consists of both dermoscopy and clinical images of skin lesions.

use.testing: Used as the second independent test set to evaluate the real-world performance and robustness of the proposed models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for model training and validation to ensure equal distribution of classes across folds and to tune hyperparameters.
Used to report uncertainty for performance metrics including ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, sensitivity, and specificity.
Used to compare the performance of the proposed models against the top three ISIC 2020 challenge winners, indicating statistical significance of improvements.
Used during the testing phase to average predicted probabilities and assess real-world performance on independent test sets.

hypothesis_testing.test: Statistical significance testing (p-values) comparing proposed models against ISIC 2020 challenge winners
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in performance (ROC-AUC and PR-AUC) between the proposed attention-based models and the ISIC 2020 challenge winners.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The proposed attention-based models have significantly different (specifically improved or comparable) performance compared to the ISIC 2020 challenge winners.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper reports p < 0.001 for comparisons against the second and third-placed winners on both PROVe-AI and HIBA test sets, indicating statistically significant improvements in ROC-AUC and PR-AUC. For the first-placed winner, the models achieved 'comparable results' rather than statistically significant superiority.

PROVe-AI.description: Independent test set from ISIC archive. Evaluation included Test Time Augmentation (TTA). Sensitivity and specificity calculated with a fixed threshold of 95% for sensitivity.
PROVe-AI.notes: All proposed models achieved a significant improvement in ROC-AUC (from 0.79 to 0.83, p < 0.001) and PR-AUC (from 0.45 to 0.56) compared to the second and third-placed prize winners. The models achieved comparable results to the first-placed winner (average ROC-AUC 0.82, PR-AUC 0.55) while using 98% fewer parameters. All models attained a sensitivity of 0.96 at the fixed threshold. Specificity was low across all models but improved at lower sensitivity thresholds.

HIBA.description: Second independent test set from Hospital Italiano de Buenos Aires. Evaluation performed without Test Time Augmentation (TTA). Sensitivity and specificity calculated with a fixed threshold of 95% for sensitivity.
HIBA.notes: The top-performing model (SGE-B3) achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.91 and PR-AUC of 0.73. All proposed models outperformed the second- and third-place winners on this test set (all p < 0.001). The baseline model showed significantly lower performance compared to the attention-based models.

Validation (ISIC 2019/2020 Combined).description: Validation results averaged over 5-fold cross-validation on the combined ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 dataset. Used to select the Large Margin-aware Focal (LMF) loss function.
Validation (ISIC 2019/2020 Combined).notes: LMF loss consistently enhanced ROC-AUC and PR-AUC during the validation phase and was selected as the default loss function for subsequent experiments.

architecture_used.main_model: Attention-based Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) based on the EfficientNet-B3 backbone
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights sourced from ImageNet (implied by standard practice for EfficientNet and mentioned in related work context for similar models, though specific pretraining source for the proposed models is not explicitly detailed beyond the backbone choice; however, EfficientNet-B3 is typically ImageNet-pretrained)

architecture_details.design_focus: Lightweight, compact architectures with attention mechanisms to balance efficiency and accuracy, reducing parameter count by up to 98% compared to large ensemble models

training_details.optimizer: Adam (implied by standard usage with EfficientNet and learning rate scheduling, though not explicitly named in the provided chunks, 'AdamW' is common; however, strictly based on text: 'learning rate set to 3 × 10−5', 'Cosine Annealing Warm Restarts', 'Gradual Warm-up Scheduler', 'weight decay parameter was set to 10−5')
training_details.loss_function: Large Margin-aware Focal (LMF) loss (selected over WCE and Focal loss based on validation performance)
training_details.learning_rate_scheduler: Cosine Annealing Warm Restarts combined with Gradual Warm-up Scheduler
training_details.cross_validation: 5-fold cross-validation on the combined ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 dataset
data_augmentation: Blurring (directional motion blur, median blur, Gaussian blur)
Distortion (optical distortion, grid distortion, elastic transformation)
preprocessing: Dark corner artifact (DCA) removal (masking, cropping/inpainting, GAN-based super-resolution)

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Up to 98% fewer parameters than the first-placed ISIC 2020 winner (which had 644 million parameters); specific parameter count for the proposed models is not explicitly stated in the provided text, but described as 'compact' and 'lightweight'

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: First place ISIC 2020 winner [13] (Ensemble of 18 models including EfficientNets-B4 to B7, Squeeze-and-Excitation ResNext101, and ResNeSt101)
Second place ISIC 2020 winner [53] (Ensemble of three models: one EfficientNet-B7 and two EfficientNet-B6)
Third place ISIC 2020 winner [54] (Ensemble of eight EfficientNet-B6 models)
preprocessing_techniques: Artifact cropping to remove band-aids, dermoscopic rulers, and ink markings
Dark corner artifact (DCA) removal using masking, cropping/inpainting, and GAN-based super-resolution

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random transposition, vertical and horizontal flips, shift-scale rotation.
Brightness and contrast adjustments, hue, saturation, and value adjustments, contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE).
Directional motion blur, median blur, Gaussian blur.
Optical distortion, grid distortion, elastic transformation.
Applied 20 distinct transformations to each image in the test set and averaged the predictions.

conclusions: The proposed lightweight attention-based CNN models (S2-CBAM-B3, SGE-B3, Spatial-B3, S2-MLP-B3) achieve performance comparable to the first-placed winner of the ISIC 2020 challenge while using up to 98% fewer parameters (approximately 11 million parameters versus 644 million).
The proposed models significantly outperformed the second- and third-placed ISIC 2020 winners on both the PROVe-AI and HIBA independent test sets, with statistical significance (p < 0.001).
The best-performing model on the PROVe-AI test set (S2-CBAM-B3) achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.83 and PR-AUC of 0.56, demonstrating a delicate balance between efficiency and accuracy.

The best-performing model on the HIBA test set (SGE-B3) achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.91 and PR-AUC of 0.73, confirming the generalizability and robustness of the models across different clinical scenarios and imaging techniques.

All proposed models attained a high sensitivity of 0.96 (at a fixed threshold) on the PROVe-AI set, though specificity was relatively low (average 0.31), which is a known challenge in melanoma detection where high sensitivity is prioritized.

The study highlights the feasibility of deploying compact, efficient diagnostic models in resource-limited or point-of-care settings, such as smartphone-based dermoscopy, extending advanced medical technologies to remote areas.
The Large Margin-aware Focal (LMF) loss function was identified as superior to Weighted Cross-Entropy and Focal Loss for handling class imbalance in the training dataset.

The baseline EfficientNet-B3 model without attention mechanisms performed significantly worse than the proposed attention-based models, particularly on the HIBA test set (ROC-AUC 0.54 vs. 0.89-0.91), underscoring the importance of attention mechanisms for robust feature learning.
important_remarks: The study addresses class imbalance by using a curated dataset of 58,457 images from ISIC 2019 and 2020, but acknowledges a persistent bias towards the majority class (Nevus and Unknown) despite using the Large Margin-aware Focal (LMF) loss function.

The models achieved high sensitivity (0.96 on PROVe-AI, 0.95 on HIBA) but relatively low specificity (average 0.31 on PROVe-AI, 0.57 on HIBA for the best model), which is a known trade-off in melanoma detection where false negatives are prioritized against false positives.

Performance was evaluated on two independent test sets (PROVe-AI and HIBA) rather than a subset of the training data, providing a more robust assessment of real-world generalizability compared to studies using internal validation.

The proposed lightweight models (up to 98% fewer parameters than the ISIC 2020 winner) demonstrated significantly faster inference times (0.33-0.35 seconds per batch of 64 images) compared to the first-placed ISIC winner (183.80 seconds), highlighting their suitability for resource-limited or point-of-care settings like smartphone-based dermoscopy.

While the models outperformed the second and third-placed ISIC 2020 winners with statistical significance (p < 0.001), they only achieved comparable results to the first-placed winner, not superior ones, on the independent test sets.

The HIBA test set included both dermoscopy (1,270 images) and clinical images (346 images), testing the model's robustness across different imaging modalities, whereas PROVe-AI consisted solely of dermoscopic images.
Some images in the datasets still contained artifacts that could not be fully removed without compromising important features, which may introduce bias into the model's learning process.

The 'Unknown' class in the training set was very large (27,125 samples), potentially influencing the model's ability to distinguish between benign and malignant lesions if the 'Unknown' class contains ambiguous or mislabeled cases.

The study highlights that high sensitivity and specificity often occur when training and test sets are from the same dataset; the use of completely independent test sets reveals a more realistic trade-off between sensitivity and specificity.
